metadata.title: How Nine Numbers Describe All of Three-Dimensional Space
metadata.color_semantics: TEAL = basis vectors landing / matrix columns; CRIMSON = arbitrary vector / classical confusion
metadata.note: Exclusions honored: no determinant, no eigenvalues, no matrix inversion. Wonder register.

beats.narration_text: med davy A-I. From Sanskrit — meaning intelligent, or intellectually brilliant. The perfect name for our A-I-powered intelligent learning systems.

props.headline: Medhavy AI
props.body:
मेधावी (Medhavy): From Sanskrit, meaning
"intelligent" or "intellectually brilliant"
— the perfect name for our AI-powered
intelligent learning systems.

beats.narration_text: Every rotation in a video game, every robot arm movement, every 3D render is secretly nine numbers multiplying a vector. Nine numbers describe all of three-dimensional space.

card.copy: How Nine Numbers Describe All of Three-Dimensional Space

beats.narration_text: A linear transformation moves every point in 3D space — infinitely many points. How does a finite list of nine numbers tell every point where to go?

production_viz.label: 3D grid of points with arrows showing transformation; question mark over the nine numbers

beats.narration_text: Linear transformations have one special property: they preserve grid lines and keep the origin fixed. If you know where the corner of each grid square lands, you know where every point lands.

card.sub: Linear transformations preserve grid lines — and that changes everything

beats.narration_text: There are three basis vectors: î equals (1,0,0), ĵ equals (0,1,0), k̂ equals (0,0,1). Every other vector is a weighted sum of these. Knowing where î, ĵ, and k̂ land is knowing everything — because linearity does the rest.

production_viz.label: Three basis vectors i-hat, j-hat, k-hat in TEAL shown in 3D; arbitrary vector as weighted sum

beats.narration_text: Where î, ĵ, k̂ land is three vectors of three components each — nine numbers. Write them as the columns of a 3×3 matrix. The matrix encodes the transformation completely.

production_viz.label: Three landing vectors assembling column by column into a 3x3 matrix; TEAL column highlights

beats.narration_text: To apply the transformation to any vector (x, y, z): the result is x times the first column plus y times the second column plus z times the third. This is matrix-vector multiplication — not a rule from nowhere, but a direct consequence of linearity.

production_viz.label: Matrix-vector multiplication shown as weighted column sum; x, y, z weights in CRIMSON; columns in TEAL

beats.narration_text: A 90° rotation around the Y-axis: î goes to (0,0,−1), ĵ goes to (0,1,0), k̂ goes to (1,0,0). The matrix has these as its columns. Apply to (2,−1,3): 2 times (0,0,−1) plus −1 times (0,1,0) plus 3 times (1,0,0) equals (3,−1,−2).

production_viz.label: Y-axis rotation matrix with columns; worked example (2,-1,3) → (3,-1,-2); check mark

beats.narration_text: Composing two transformations is matrix multiplication: first one transformation moves the basis vectors, then a second transformation moves them again. The resulting nine numbers describe the combined action without needing to track every point.

production_viz.label: Two matrices multiplying; basis vectors moving twice; combined result matrix

beats.narration_text: Three dimensions need nine numbers — three basis vectors times three components. Two dimensions need four. N dimensions need N squared. The pattern is exact: linearity makes a finite description of an infinite space possible.

production_viz.label: Table: 2D→4 numbers, 3D→9 numbers, ND→N^2 numbers; TEAL highlight on pattern

beats.narration_text: A linear transformation equals where the three basis vectors land. Write those landing positions as columns. Multiply any vector by that matrix. Nine numbers, all of 3D space.
med davy. A-I-powered intelligent learning systems. On YouTube, at med davy A-I. med davy dot com.
